content.text: What Conversation Flow Wireframes Are

A conversation flow wireframe is a low-fidelity representation of an agent's dialogue before any code is written. Unlike a UI wireframe that sketches screen layout, a conversation wireframe sketches the sequence of turns between a user and an agent: what the user says, what the agent responds, what information is needed before the agent can respond, and where the flow branches when something goes wrong. The artifact is typically a three-column table — user turn, agent turn, design note — arranged in time order from the opening utterance to session close.

Conversation wireframes serve a similar function in agent design as mockups do in UI design: they make the intended experience concrete enough to review, critique, and revise before engineering begins, at a cost measured in hours rather than sprints. A conversation wireframe session typically runs two hours and produces a complete script prototype covering the happy path and the two or three most important failure branches. That two-hour session prevents weeks of prompt rework because it surfaces design disagreements — about tone, scope, clarification strategy, error handling — while they are still cheap to resolve.
The Three-Column Script Format

The most widely used format for conversation wireframes is the three-column script table. The diagram above shows this format in action for a meeting-booking agent. Each row represents one turn-exchange (user utterance followed by agent response). The third column captures the design note that explains the branch logic: what information is missing, why this particular response was chosen, and what alternative paths exist from this point.

attrs.alt: Conversation Flow Wireframe — Three-Column Script Format
attrs.title: Conversation Flow Wireframe

content.text: The three-column format has several advantages over alternative representations. First, it is readable by non-technical stakeholders — product owners, business sponsors, and conversation designers can all review it without understanding prompt engineering. Second, it makes branch points explicit through the design note column, which specifies the trigger condition that causes the flow to deviate from the row above. Third, it maps directly to eval scenarios: each failure branch row in the wireframe becomes a negative test case in the eval suite, and each happy-path row becomes a positive test case.
What to Include in Each Column

The user turn column should be written in the user's actual voice — not sanitised into a clean requirement sentence. If users commonly say "book a meeting with the team next Tuesday," write exactly that, not "schedule team meeting for next Tuesday." The gap between how users actually phrase requests and how designers imagine they phrase them is a significant source of slot-filling failures in production agents. The wireframe that uses real user voice catches these gaps during design.

User turn: the verbatim user utterance, as realistic as possible. Include typos or informal phrasing if the target user population commonly uses them. Mark intentionally ambiguous utterances with [AMBIGUOUS] to flag them for the conversation designer.

Agent turn: the agent's ideal response in plain prose. This is not the system prompt — it is what the agent should say, written by a conversation designer. Do not include technical implementation details ("I'll call the calendar API"). Do include the exact phrasing of clarification questions and error messages, because these are hard to get right and benefit from early review.

Design note: the branch logic annotation. Use a consistent notation: MISSING SLOT (when the user hasn't provided required information), FAILURE (when something goes wrong), BRANCH (when the flow diverges based on an external condition), and CONFIRM (when the agent needs explicit user confirmation before proceeding). Each annotation maps to a corresponding entry in the failure mode register.
Script Prototypes: From Wireframe to Dialogue

A script prototype is a fully written out multi-turn conversation, without the branch table structure of a wireframe. Script prototypes are used for two purposes: stakeholder walkthroughs and Wizard of Oz testing. For stakeholder walkthroughs, the script is read aloud in a meeting — one person plays the user, another plays the agent — to give the audience a visceral sense of the intended experience that a table format cannot provide. For Wizard of Oz testing, the script is used by a human operator who responds to real test users' messages manually, simulating the agent's behaviour before it exists.

A script prototype for a single task flow covers the happy path end-to-end at a level of detail that reveals every design decision: exactly what the agent says at each turn, exactly how it responds to each failure mode, and exactly how it closes the session. The prototype should be long enough to feel like a real conversation — typically five to twelve turns for a moderately complex task — but should not include every possible branch. The wireframe handles branch coverage; the script handles experience depth.
A Complete Script Prototype Example

SCRIPT PROTOTYPE — Meeting Booking Agent
Scenario: Employee books a team meeting with a conflict
Persona: P-01 (Power User, familiar with the agent)

[Turn 1 — Opening]
User: "Book a meeting with the design team next Tuesday around 2pm"
Agent: "Let me check everyone's availability for Tuesday 2pm...
       [checking]
       Alex Chen is busy from 2-3pm on Tuesday. Would 3pm work
       for the team? Or I can check Wednesday if you prefer."
Design note: calendar lookup returns conflict; agent proactively offers alternatives
             rather than just reporting the conflict and stopping

[Turn 2 — Constraint resolution]
User: "Is 3pm free for everyone?"
Agent: "Yes, all four design team members are free at 3pm on Tuesday.
       Should I book it?"
Design note: agent confirms full availability before asking for confirmation;
             confirmation required because booking is an irreversible action

[Turn 3 — Confirmation]
User: "Yes go ahead"
Agent: "Booked. Team Meeting is on Tuesday 15 July at 3pm.
       I've sent calendar invites to Alex, Sam, Jordan, and Priya.
       Would you like to add an agenda or dial-in link?"
Design note: confirmation includes all booked participants by name;
             proactive offer to add agenda (upsell of related capability)

[Turn 4 — Optional extension]
User: "Add the Zoom link from yesterday's standup"
Agent: "I can add a Zoom link, but I don't have access to your previous
       meeting links. Could you paste the Zoom URL here?"
Design note: agent cannot retrieve historical meeting links — honest capability boundary;
             requests the specific piece it needs rather than saying 'I can't do that'

[Turn 5 — Close]
User: "https://zoom.us/j/12345678"
Agent: "Done. I've added that Zoom link to the Tuesday 3pm meeting.
       Is there anything else you need for this meeting?"
Design note: session close question; allows user to add more details or end session

[Turn 6 — Close]
User: "That's all thanks"
Agent: "Great. Have a good meeting on Tuesday!"
Design note: brief, warm close; no summary repeat (user confirmed everything earlier)

--- FAILURE BRANCH SCRIPTS ---

[Failure: all slots present but no availability found]
Agent: "I couldn't find a time this week when all four design team
       members are free for a 30-minute slot. Would you like me to
       check next week, or would a smaller group work?"
Design note: graceful failure with two alternatives; agent does not give up

[Failure: user gives ambiguous team name]
User: "Book a meeting with the design team"
Agent: "I found two groups called 'design team' in your org:
       - Product Design (Alex, Sam, Jordan, Priya)
       - Brand Design (Marcus, Leila)
       Which team should I book with?"
Design note: disambiguation required; agent lists both with members for clarity

[Failure: calendar API timeout]
Agent: "I'm having trouble reaching the calendar right now.
       I've saved your request. Try again in a minute, or check
       your calendar directly at [calendar URL]."
Design note: graceful degradation; request preserved; direct link provided
Wizard of Oz Prototyping for Agent Flows

Wizard of Oz prototyping is a research technique where a human operator (the 'wizard') simulates the agent's responses in real time to real test users who believe they are interacting with an automated system. The technique originated in HCI research in the 1980s and is particularly well-suited to conversational AI because it allows the team to test conversation flows with real users before any model or prompt work is done.

To run a Wizard of Oz session for an agent flow: prepare the script prototype as a reference document for the wizard. The wizard needs a lookup table mapping common user utterance patterns to the scripted agent responses. For utterances not covered by the script, the wizard uses their judgment guided by the behaviour spec. A second team member observes and takes notes on where users deviate from the expected script — these deviations become design revisions in the wireframe.
Run sessions of 15-20 minutes with 5-8 users. Fewer than 5 produces insufficient signal; more than 8 yields diminishing returns before the first design revision.

Use a two-channel setup: users type in a chat interface they believe is automated; the wizard reads the messages and types agent responses from a separate window. No video required — the key data is the text transcript.

After each session, debrief immediately: what did the user say that the wizard had to improvise? Those improvisations become new rows in the wireframe. What caused the user to pause or seem confused? Those moments become friction-score annotations.

After three sessions, revise the script prototype before running the remaining sessions. A wireframe that incorporates real user deviations produces a significantly more robust specification than one based entirely on design assumptions.
Wire-Flow Diagrams: Visualising Branch Logic

For complex agent flows with many branches, the three-column table becomes difficult to read horizontally. A wire-flow diagram represents the same information as a directed graph: each node is an agent state (waiting for user input, processing, delivering output), and each edge is a transition triggered by a user utterance or an internal condition. The diagram makes the branching structure visually apparent and is easier to review in stakeholder presentations.

Wire-flow diagrams are most useful when: the flow has more than five branch points, the flow contains loops (the agent can return to an earlier state), or the flow involves parallel paths (the agent simultaneously pursues multiple sub-goals). For simpler flows, the three-column script table is adequate and faster to produce. Reserve wire-flow diagrams for flows that would require three or more pages of script to represent linearly.

flowchart TD
    A([User: book meeting]) --> B{Time specified?}
    B -- yes --> C{Date specified?}
    B -- no --> D[Ask: when?]
    D --> C
    C -- yes --> E[Check calendar availability]
    C -- no --> F[Ask: which day?]
    F --> E
    E --> G{All free?}
    G -- yes --> H[Confirm with user]
    G -- no --> I[Offer alternatives]
    I --> J{User accepts alt?}
    J -- yes --> H
    J -- no --> K[Offer different week or smaller group]
    H --> L[Book + send invites]
    L --> M[Confirm booked — offer agenda add]
    M --> N([Close])
    E --> O{API error?}
    O -- timeout --> P[Save draft, inform user]
    O -- auth_error --> Q[Escalate: contact IT]
Translating Wireframes Into Technical Specifications

The conversation wireframe is not the final output — it is the input to the technical specification that engineers implement. The translation from wireframe to technical spec involves three steps: extracting slot definitions, defining intent categories, and specifying failure handling.

Slot extraction: every MISSING SLOT annotation in the wireframe becomes a slot definition in the technical spec. For each slot: name, type, required or optional, the question the agent asks to fill it, maximum fill attempts, and what the agent does after exhausting fill attempts. This slot catalogue is the direct input to the agent's tool parameter schema (PRD section 8).

Intent category definition: each distinct user utterance pattern in the wireframe represents an intent. Cataloguing the intents from the wireframe — book-meeting, check-availability, modify-booking, cancel-booking — gives the conversation designer a complete list of intents the agent must handle, which drives the intent-classification component of the system prompt.

Failure handling specification: each FAILURE annotation in the wireframe maps to a specific failure mode in the failure register (PRD section 10). The wireframe specifies the user-facing language for the failure; the failure register specifies the mitigation tier and the eval scenario.

Confirmation flow specification: each CONFIRM annotation in the wireframe maps to a confirmation requirement in the prompt requirements (PRD section 7). The wireframe specifies what the confirmation asks; the prompt requirements specify when it is triggered.

Memory requirement extraction: slots that must persist across turns (the team name confirmed in turn 1 must still be remembered in turn 4) are memory requirements extracted from the wireframe and documented in the PRD's context management section.
Tools and Formats for Conversation Wireframing

The most effective tool for conversation wireframing is the one the team will actually use. Fancy tooling does not produce better wireframes — disciplined process does. That said, certain tools have advantages for specific use cases.

Google Sheets or Airtable: the fastest format for the three-column script table. All stakeholders can view and comment without installing software. Export to PDF for PRD appendix. Limitation: branch visualisation requires a separate tool.

Miro or FigJam: best for wire-flow diagrams and branching visualisation. Supports colour-coding by failure type, linking rows to design notes, and exporting to PDF. Good for stakeholder presentations. Limitation: the table format for scripts is less natural than a spreadsheet.

Markdown in repository: best for teams that want to version-control their wireframes alongside code. The three-column table renders as a Markdown table. Mermaid diagrams render natively in GitHub. Limitation: real-time collaboration is harder than cloud document tools.

Physical sticky notes on a whiteboard: best for the initial discovery session where the team is still figuring out the flow. Fast to create, easy to rearrange, supports rich visual annotation. Limitation: must be transcribed to a digital format before the session ends or the artefact is lost.
Common Mistakes in Conversation Wireframing

Writing only the happy path. A wireframe with no failure branches is a demo script, not a specification. Always include at least two failure branches — the most likely failure and the most damaging failure.

Using sanitised language in the user turn column. Writing "I would like to schedule a meeting" when real users say "book a meeting with the team" produces a wireframe that misleads the conversation designer about the vocabulary and structure of real utterances.

Skipping Wizard of Oz testing. Teams that write the wireframe without testing it with users discover its flaws in the eval suite — at engineering cost — rather than in a two-hour research session. Wizard of Oz sessions are cheap; revising a shipped agent's conversational design is not.

Not extracting technical requirements. A wireframe that stays in the design folder without being translated into slot definitions, intent categories, and failure register entries has not completed its job. The translation is the product manager's responsibility and must happen before sprint planning.

Making the wireframe too detailed too early. A first-pass wireframe should take two hours, not two days. Use rough, natural language in the agent turn column — leave the exact phrasing to the conversation designer. A wireframe that takes too long to create will be skipped in time-pressured sprints.
Summary

Conversation flow wireframes and script prototypes are the lo-fi design artifacts that make an agent's intended dialogue concrete and reviewable before engineering begins. The three-column script format — user turn, agent turn, design note — captures both the happy path and failure branches in a format that all stakeholders can review. Script prototypes provide depth for Wizard of Oz testing and stakeholder walkthroughs. Wire-flow diagrams visualise branch structure for complex flows. Together, these artifacts transform the abstract behaviour specification in the PRD into a tangible dialogue design that conversation designers can implement and QA can test — preventing the most expensive class of agent design errors: misunderstood conversational behaviour discovered in production.
